feedback.text: Im writing to express my deep concern and that of many others in the gaming community about a troubling trend: game publishers removing games from stores and game libraries, even after the consumers have already paid the full price for them. This practice, often called "killing a game," undermines consumer rights and feels fundamentally unfair. The Stop Killing Games movement is gaining momentum across Europe and globally, highlighting this critical issue. To prevent this, publishers should be required to create end-of-life plans for their games when they intend to cease support. These plans should prioritize a smooth transition from online dependencies to full offline functionality. Where thats not feasible, publishers should provide the necessary tools and resources for community-hosted servers, allowing players to continue enjoying the game independently. I believe these changes could safeguard consumer rights, preserve valuable cultural assets, and foster a more sustainable ecosystem for the video game industry. Thank you for your time and consideration.
